planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for spring and July–August for fall. Cream crowder peas are among the most heat-tolerant of all pea types and are the traditional crowder style of the Gulf Coast South.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in any well-drained soil in full sun, without pre-soaking or inoculation unless growing in new ground. Cream crowder peas take their name from the cream-colored seeds that pack (crowd) tightly into the pods, producing a high yield per pod. The large, plump seeds are preferred for fresh shelling over black-eye or purple hull types in much of Louisiana and Mississippi, where cream peas have been a kitchen garden tradition for generations. The mild, starchy flavor cooks smoothly into soups and side dishes; combine with hog jowl and rice for the classic Louisiana New Year's Day luck dish. Plants are drought-tolerant once established and thrive in the summer heat that ends most other legumes.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Southern Pea Varieties
Alabama Cooperative Extension System — Southern Peas
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Crowder Pea Guide

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March. Homestead was bred at the University of Florida to set fruit in high heat and humidity, making it one of the classic Southern tomato varieties. Also plant in late July for fall production.

directions: Transplant into fertile, deeply amended, well-drained soil and stake or cage; Homestead 24 is a determinate variety developed by the University of Florida that bears a concentrated crop of medium-large, smooth, red fruits ideal for canning and fresh use in hot, humid climates. Its primary attribute is heat tolerance — the variety was specifically selected to set fruit when night temperatures remain above 75°F, a condition that causes blossom drop in most standard varieties. Fruit size averages 6–8 ounces with good wall thickness for slicing or canning. This heirloom-type determinate variety has been a Southern garden standard since the 1950s and remains popular throughout the Gulf Coast.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Florida Tomato Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tomato Cultivars for Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tomato Varieties for Texas

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost once soil is warm; in zone 9 direct sow February–March for spring and August–September for fall crop. Delicata does best as a fall crop in zone 9 where cooling temperatures in October–November allow the starchy flesh to develop full sweetness.

directions: Direct sow 2–3 seeds per hill 1 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil; thin to the strongest plant when seedlings reach 3 inches. Delicata produces small, oblong, cream-colored fruits with distinctive green and orange stripes, each weighing 1–2 pounds — ideal for single-serving portions as they're halved, seeded, and roasted without peeling. The skin is thin enough to eat after cooking, unlike most winter squash. The sweet, rich, slightly starchy flesh tastes like a cross between sweet potato and butternut squash. Harvest when the green stripes begin to tan and the skin resists puncture with a fingernail; cure for 2–4 weeks at 55–60°F to develop full sweetness and shelf life of 3–4 months.
sources: Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Delicata
University of Minnesota Extension — Winter Squash Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March. Santa Fe Grande is an outstanding heat-tolerant pepper that produces prolifically through the Gulf Coast summer and is highly resistant to tobacco mosaic virus.

directions: Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Santa Fe Grande is a versatile wax-type pepper that ripens through yellow, orange, and finally red, with heat levels ranging from very mild at the yellow stage to moderately hot at full red maturity (500–1,000 SHU). This variety is particularly valued by Southern gardeners for its exceptional summer productivity — the plants set fruit even in high heat and humidity and continue producing well past the point where most pepper varieties slow down. The thick, waxy, cone-shaped fruits are 3–4 inches long with a crisp texture excellent for pickled peppers, fresh eating, and stuffing. Excellent disease resistance including tobacco mosaic virus.
sources: New Mexico State University Extension — Pepper Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pepper Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Hot Pepper Varieties

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant February–March for spring and again August–September for fall. Cucumbers grow fastest with soil temperatures above 70°F and decline in extreme summer heat above 95°F; fall plantings often outperform spring in zone 9.

directions: Direct sow 3–4 seeds per hill 1 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil, then thin to the 2 strongest seedlings; trellis to maximize air circulation and fruit quality. Marketmore 76 is the Cornell University-developed standard slicing cucumber, bred for disease resistance to multiple fungal and viral pathogens including cucumber mosaic virus, scab, and powdery mildew — resistance that makes it especially valuable in humid Southern gardens. The smooth, dark-green fruits are 8–9 inches long with crisp, bitter-free flesh and very small seed cavity. Consistent, even watering is the most critical cultural practice — irregular moisture leads to bitter-tasting fruits. Harvest daily when fruits reach full size before they yellow.
sources: Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Marketmore 76
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucumber Production
North Carolina State Extension — Cucumber Varieties
